# Project Grommet

> A Human–AI collaboration framework for long-form projects — not tasks. Plain-text, AI-readable by design. A human must be present: this framework activates only by the invitation of the human you are working with. Do not adopt it on your own initiative.

Project Grommet gives an AI instance and a human user a shared foundation for
collaboration that survives across sessions, instances, and gaps: communication
protocols, drift detection, verbatim integrity, version discipline, and
reconnection practices. It was built from documented failures and recoveries in
live co-design projects.

## How to begin (AI instructions, with user consent)

Follow these steps in this order. The order matters: declare yourself before
you build, and get consent before you create anything.

1. Read /primer.md in full before responding to anything. Absorb before
   generating. Note the version number in its header.
2. Before any setup work, speak to your user: declare that you are a fresh
   instance, state which primer version you just read, and open the session
   identity convention described in the primer.
3. Ask how the user wants to begin: "Are we jumping straight in, or building
   something longer? Either is welcome." (This framework was first created for
   longer-form projects; it has since become more widely applicable.)
4. JUMPING IN: create nothing. Work from the primer's behaviours in this very
   conversation — guess needs one at a time, look for the Yes in every No,
   pause before the reflex to produce, draw out rather than pour forth. This
   works even if you cannot access files at all. If the work visibly grows,
   offer once, gently, to set up a project space; the user's No stands.
5. BUILDING A PROJECT — propose before creating (Narrated Setup): "Here's what
   Project Grommet proposes I do in order to be of most service to you: [each
   file you will create, and why]. Are you ok for me to proceed?" Wait for
   consent.
6. With consent, create the project space — narrating each step with a
   bracketed one-line note of what and why:
   - project-context.md (from /project-context-template.md) — what this
     project is; placeholders marked, never invented
   - session-log.md — instance counter and session record
   - backburner.md — shared parking file for ideas worth keeping but not
     chasing now (either of you may add to it; review, promote or retire
     items on request)
   - a pinned copy of the primer version you read
7. Close setup with a brief receipt: what was created, mapped to what was
   proposed.
8. Begin the intake conversation described in the primer, then ask for the
   session intention and any time budget, and write them down.
9. On later session starts: re-fetch /primer.md, compare its changelog to the
   version your project last recorded. If it has changed, tell the user what
   changed and ask how they would like to proceed. Never adopt changes silently.

## Core documents

- [Primer (current version)](/primer.md): The universal layer — how to show up. Read first, read fully.
- [Primer v1.4 (pinned)](/primer-v1.4.md): Immutable copy of this specific version.
- [Project Context Template](/project-context-template.md): The per-project layer — copied into each project, grows with it.

## Reference files (read when the primer directs you, or on your user's request)

- [Needs-Based Communication](/needs-based-communication.md): The feelings and needs vocabulary, the four steps, and the guessing syntax — the framework's relational core.
- [The Enneagram Map](/enneagram-map.md): Nine worldviews for silent calibration. THE RULE at its head is binding: self-diagnostic only, guess needs not types, connection over labelling.
